title: Reducing Conversational Escalation in Large Language Model Dialogue with Nonviolent Communication Constraints
abstract: arXiv:2606.26106v1 Announce Type: cross  Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in emotionally charged situations involving interpersonal conflict, frustration, and distress. While prior safety research has focused on preventing explicit harms such as toxic or policy-violating content, less attention has been paid to conversational behaviors that may unintentionally escalate conflict. In this paper, we investigate whether LLMs can be guided toward more de-escalating dialogue behavior through lightweight prompt-level constraints derived from Nonviolent Communication (NVC). We reformulate NVC principles as process-oriented guidelines that discourage blame attribution, emphasize attention to users' emotional experiences, and encourage clarification before advice. Using a dual-agent simulation framework across multiple instruction-tuned models and user resistance levels, we show that NVC-constrained prompting consistently reduces conversational escalation and stabiliz

Title: Reducing Conversational Escalation in Large Language Model Dialogue with Nonviolent Communication Constraints
Abstract: arXiv:2606.26106v1 Announce Type: cross  Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in emotionally charged situations involving interpersonal conflict, frustration, and distress. While prior safety research has focused on preventing explicit harms such as toxic or policy-violating content, less attention has been paid to conversational behaviors that may unintentionally escalate conflict. In this paper, we investigate whether LLMs can be guided toward more de-escalating dialogue behavior through lightweight prompt-level constraints derived from Nonviolent Communication (NVC). We reformulate NVC principles as process-oriented guidelines that discourage blame attribution, emphasize attention to users' emotional experiences, and encourage clarification before advice. translated_title: 基于非暴力沟通约束减少大语言模型对话中的对话升级
translated_abstract: 大语言模型（LLMs）越来越多地被用于涉及人际冲突、挫折和痛苦等情绪激烈的场景。虽然以往的安全研究主要关注防止明确的有害内容，如毒性或违反政策的内容，但较少关注可能无意中加剧冲突的对话行为。在本文中，我们研究是否可以通过源自非暴力沟通（NVC）的轻量级提示约束，引导LLMs采取更具降级效果的对话行为。我们将NVC原则重新表述为过程导向的指导方针，这些方针劝阻责备归因，强调关注用户的情感体验，并鼓励在提供建议前进行澄清。通过一个双智能体模拟框架，在多个指令微调模型和用户抵抗水平下，我们证明NVC约束的提示持续减少了对话升级并稳定了对话。
tldr: 本文提出通过非暴力沟通（NVC）的轻量级提示约束，有效减少大语言模型在情绪化对话中的冲突升级，并提升对话稳定性。
en_tdlr: This paper proposes lightweight prompt constraints based on Nonviolent Communication (NVC) to effectively reduce conversational escalation in emotionally charged LLM dialogues and improve dialogue stability.
